Exercise Description: Create variables using three different keywords. Assign values of different types to each. Investigate the differences when you try to reassign them. Reference: https://www.typescriptlang.org/docs/
Declare variables and explicitly specify their types. Try to assign a wrong type value and observe what TypeScript tells you. Reference: https://www.typescriptlang.org/docs/
Create a variable that cannot be changed after assignment. Try to reassign it and see what happens. Compare with other variable types. Reference: https://www.typescriptlang.org/docs/
Write code in different scopes (global, function, block). Find out where each variable is accessible and why some variables appear and disappear. Reference: https://www.typescriptlang.org/docs/
Create a counter using loops and increment it inside a code block. Investigate if that variable is accessible outside the block. Reference: https://www.typescriptlang.org/docs/

Declare variables at different levels (global, function, block). Show how inner scopes access outer variables and what happens when you declare same-named variables at different levels. Reference: https://www.typescriptlang.org/docs/
Create variables with different values (text, number, true/false). Find out what type each one is and how to check/discover the type in TypeScript. Reference: https://www.typescriptlang.org/docs/
Build a simple calculator that adds two numbers. Try to pass wrong types (text instead of number) and see what TypeScript catches. Reference: https://www.typescriptlang.org/docs/
Create text variables and combine them in different ways. Find the method to use values inside text without concatenation. Reference: https://www.typescriptlang.org/docs/
Create variables with different types. Use them in operations together and see what TypeScript allows or prevents. Reference: https://www.typescriptlang.org/docs/
Create functions that can handle different input types. Discover how TypeScript warns you about type mismatches. Reference: https://www.typescriptlang.org/docs/
Build a validation system that checks if data matches expected types before processing. Show what happens with wrong types. Reference: https://www.typescriptlang.org/docs/
Create an enum that represents browser types with numeric values. Access specific enum values and print them. Reference: https://www.typescriptlang.org/docs/
Create an enum with string values representing different environments. Use enum values in a variable. Reference: https://www.typescriptlang.org/docs/
Create two different enums with different value types (one numeric, one string). Show what each represents. Reference: https://www.typescriptlang.org/docs/
Create an enum with mixed configurations. Use those values to store and access application settings. Reference: https://www.typescriptlang.org/docs/
Build a system using enums to represent different states or options. Access and use those values. Reference: https://www.typescriptlang.org/docs/
Create a complex enum-based system with multiple enums. Show how to work with enum values throughout code. Reference: https://www.typescriptlang.org/docs/
Create an object with multiple properties. Access each property and print them to console. Reference: https://www.typescriptlang.org/docs/
Create an object with nested objects. Access nested properties using dot notation. Reference: https://www.typescriptlang.org/docs/
Create a type that describes an object structure. Create an object matching that type. Reference: https://www.typescriptlang.org/docs/
Create multiple objects and combine them together using spread operator. Show merged result. Reference: https://www.typescriptlang.org/docs/
Create objects matching different type definitions. Show how TypeScript enforces type matching. Reference: https://www.typescriptlang.org/docs/
Build a system with typed objects that have nested structures. Show how types validate shape and content. Reference: https://www.typescriptlang.org/docs/
Create an array and add items to it. Discover methods to add and remove items. Reference: https://www.typescriptlang.org/docs/
Create arrays with specific types. See what happens when wrong types are added. Reference: https://www.typescriptlang.org/docs/
Iterate over arrays in different ways. Discover various patterns for looping. Reference: https://www.typescriptlang.org/docs/
Create arrays and loop through them using for-of loops. Discover how to access each element. Reference: https://www.typescriptlang.org/docs/
Build functions that work with arrays. Add items, remove items, and check array lengths. Reference: https://www.typescriptlang.org/docs/
Create typed arrays and use multiple push/pop operations. Show how TypeScript enforces consistent types. Reference: https://www.typescriptlang.org/docs/
Work with text and discover methods to combine strings and change their case. Reference: https://www.typescriptlang.org/docs/
Create dynamic strings that combine variables with text. Discover a special syntax for this. Reference: https://www.typescriptlang.org/docs/
Search within strings to find specific text or patterns. Discover different search methods. Reference: https://www.typescriptlang.org/docs/
Build utilities that transform and manipulate strings. Find methods to split, join, and clean text. Reference: https://www.typescriptlang.org/docs/
Validate string patterns without regex. Discover methods to check what a string contains. Reference: https://www.typescriptlang.org/docs/
Build a string processing system that validates and transforms text data. Reference: https://www.typescriptlang.org/docs/
Write a function that takes input values and produces output. Find how to specify what types go in and what type comes out. Reference: https://www.typescriptlang.org/docs/
Create functions where some parameters might not always be provided. Discover how to make parameters optional and what default values do. Reference: https://www.typescriptlang.org/docs/
Write functions in two different ways (traditional and arrow syntax). Compare the differences and find when to use each. Reference: https://www.typescriptlang.org/docs/
Create multiple functions with different input and output types. Find how TypeScript helps when you pass wrong types to functions. Reference: https://www.typescriptlang.org/docs/
Create arrow functions and regular functions side by side. Show they work the same and compare their syntax. Reference: https://www.typescriptlang.org/docs/
Build functions with different parameter combinations. Show how optional and default parameters work together. Reference: https://www.typescriptlang.org/docs/
Repeat code multiple times. Discover different loop structures and their differences. Reference: https://www.typescriptlang.org/docs/
Loop through arrays and objects. Find different ways to access items during iteration. Reference: https://www.typescriptlang.org/docs/
Create loops inside loops. Discover how they interact and what they generate. Reference: https://www.typescriptlang.org/docs/
Stop loops early or skip iterations. Discover keywords to control loop behavior. Reference: https://www.typescriptlang.org/docs/
Compare different loop approaches. Discover when to use each method. Reference: https://www.typescriptlang.org/docs/
Implement complex loop patterns and understand when loops can be replaced with other approaches. Reference: https://www.typescriptlang.org/docs/
Make code decisions based on conditions. Discover how to run different code based on true/false. Reference: https://www.typescriptlang.org/docs/
Create multiple conditions to match different cases. Discover an alternative to nested if-else. Reference: https://www.typescriptlang.org/docs/
Create quick decisions for simple conditions. Find a shorthand for if-else. Reference: https://www.typescriptlang.org/docs/
Build complex decision logic with multiple conditions. Discover how to combine conditions. Reference: https://www.typescriptlang.org/docs/
Simplify code by catching errors early with guard patterns. Refactor complex conditions. Reference: https://www.typescriptlang.org/docs/
Create sophisticated conditional patterns that handle multiple scenarios effectively. Reference: https://www.typescriptlang.org/docs/
Create a switch statement that matches different string values. Add a default case for unmatched values. Reference: https://www.typescriptlang.org/docs/
Create a switch statement with multiple cases and break statements. Show what happens when breaks are missing. Reference: https://www.typescriptlang.org/docs/
Compare a switch statement with multiple if-else statements. Show the differences. Reference: https://www.typescriptlang.org/docs/
Create a switch statement that handles different input types. Show how TypeScript checks types. Reference: https://www.typescriptlang.org/docs/
Build a switch-based system for handling different command or state values. Use break to control flow. Reference: https://www.typescriptlang.org/docs/
Create complex switch logic with nested conditions. Show proper use of break and default cases. Reference: https://www.typescriptlang.org/docs/
Do mathematical calculations. Discover the symbols used for different operations. Reference: https://www.typescriptlang.org/docs/
Compare values to get true or false. Discover the difference between similar operators. Reference: https://www.typescriptlang.org/docs/
Combine conditions with true/false logic. Discover operators that work with conditions. Reference: https://www.typescriptlang.org/docs/
Build expressions combining multiple operators. Discover how order matters. Reference: https://www.typescriptlang.org/docs/
Understand when different operators apply and how they interact. Create logic expressions. Reference: https://www.typescriptlang.org/docs/
Create complex expressions and understand operator precedence and performance. Reference: https://www.typescriptlang.org/docs/
Create arrays with fixed structure where each position has a different type. Access items by position. Reference: https://www.typescriptlang.org/docs/
Extract values from tuples into separate variables. Discover syntax for this extraction. Reference: https://www.typescriptlang.org/docs/
Create a function that returns multiple values in a specific order. Pass them to another function. Reference: https://www.typescriptlang.org/docs/
Build utility functions that create and work with tuples. Discover patterns for tuple manipulation. Reference: https://www.typescriptlang.org/docs/
Use tuples in function returns to describe what's returned. Extract values multiple ways. Reference: https://www.typescriptlang.org/docs/
Create advanced tuple patterns and handle variable-length tuples safely. Reference: https://www.typescriptlang.org/docs/
Create types that accept multiple different types. Discover how to determine which type you have. Reference: https://www.typescriptlang.org/docs/
Create functions that accept different types. Find how to handle each type safely. Reference: https://www.typescriptlang.org/docs/
Use specific string values as types. Discover how to restrict values to a list. Reference: https://www.typescriptlang.org/docs/
Build flexible systems that handle different data types. Discover patterns for safe handling. Reference: https://www.typescriptlang.org/docs/
Create objects where the content depends on a type field. Discover how TypeScript helps with these. Reference: https://www.typescriptlang.org/docs/
Implement complex type checking and manipulation patterns for flexible systems. Reference: https://www.typescriptlang.org/docs/
Create functions that work with operations that take time. Wait for them to complete. Reference: https://www.typescriptlang.org/docs/
Handle operations that don't complete immediately. Discover how to write code that waits. Reference: https://www.typescriptlang.org/docs/
Create multiple operations that happen in order. Watch how they wait for each other. Reference: https://www.typescriptlang.org/docs/
Handle errors when async operations fail. Discover how to catch problems. Reference: https://www.typescriptlang.org/docs/
Create async functions that perform multiple sequential operations. Show how each await waits for the previous one. Reference: https://www.typescriptlang.org/docs/
Build sophisticated async patterns for complex workflows. Reference: https://www.typescriptlang.org/docs/
Create a blueprint for an object with properties and behaviors. Instantiate it and use its properties and methods. Reference: https://www.typescriptlang.org/docs/
Create a class that automatically sets up properties when you create a new instance. Find the special method that runs automatically. Reference: https://www.typescriptlang.org/docs/
Create methods inside a class that use the object's own data. Discover the keyword that refers to the current object. Reference: https://www.typescriptlang.org/docs/
Create a class where some data should be private (hidden) and some public (visible). Find how to control access to properties. Reference: https://www.typescriptlang.org/docs/
Create methods that work with class properties. Show how this keyword refers to the current object's properties. Reference: https://www.typescriptlang.org/docs/
Create shared methods and properties that belong to the class itself, not instances. Discover how to access them without creating objects. Reference: https://www.typescriptlang.org/docs/
Create a contract that describes what properties and methods an object should have. Create a class that follows this contract. Reference: https://www.typescriptlang.org/docs/
Use an interface to specify what parameters a function should accept. Find how TypeScript enforces this contract. Reference: https://www.typescriptlang.org/docs/
Create an interface that extends another interface. Discover how to build on existing contracts. Reference: https://www.typescriptlang.org/docs/
Create multiple interfaces describing different object shapes. Implement a class that follows each interface. Reference: https://www.typescriptlang.org/docs/
Make a single class follow multiple interfaces. Discover how to implement multiple contracts. Reference: https://www.typescriptlang.org/docs/
Design interfaces for complex scenarios. Show how interfaces create a contract that multiple different classes can fulfill. Reference: https://www.typescriptlang.org/docs/
Create a parent class and child class. Discover which properties and methods the child automatically has. Reference: https://www.typescriptlang.org/docs/
Change a method in the child class to behave differently. Discover how to call the parent's version of the method. Reference: https://www.typescriptlang.org/docs/
Build three classes where each extends the previous one. Discover how properties flow through the hierarchy. Reference: https://www.typescriptlang.org/docs/
Create child classes that properly initialize parent data. Discover the special method needed for this. Reference: https://www.typescriptlang.org/docs/
Override methods in child classes and show different behavior. Find how to access parent methods from child. Reference: https://www.typescriptlang.org/docs/
Build complex inheritance hierarchies with proper initialization and method override patterns. Reference: https://www.typescriptlang.org/docs/
Hide internal data in a class. Create public methods to access that data safely. Reference: https://www.typescriptlang.org/docs/
Create special methods that allow controlled access to hidden data. Add validation when data is modified. Reference: https://www.typescriptlang.org/docs/
Use different access levels to control what's visible. Discover how TypeScript enforces these boundaries. Reference: https://www.typescriptlang.org/docs/
Design classes where internal properties depend on each other. Enforce rules to keep data consistent. Reference: https://www.typescriptlang.org/docs/
Create validation in setter methods. Show what happens when invalid data is provided. Reference: https://www.typescriptlang.org/docs/
Build classes with complex internal state. Show how encapsulation protects against invalid states. Reference: https://www.typescriptlang.org/docs/
Create a blueprint for a class that cannot be instantiated directly. Create child classes that implement it. Reference: https://www.typescriptlang.org/docs/
Define methods that child classes must implement. Discover how TypeScript enforces this requirement. Reference: https://www.typescriptlang.org/docs/
Create a partially implemented parent class with some abstract methods. Show how child classes complete the implementation. Reference: https://www.typescriptlang.org/docs/
Build abstract classes with multiple abstract methods. Create child classes implementing different behaviors. Reference: https://www.typescriptlang.org/docs/
Design an abstract factory that creates different types of objects. Discover how abstraction enables flexibility. Reference: https://www.typescriptlang.org/docs/
Create abstract hierarchies that define contracts at different levels. Show how abstraction guides implementation. Reference: https://www.typescriptlang.org/docs/
Create multiple classes implementing the same interface. Use them interchangeably. Reference: https://www.typescriptlang.org/docs/
Override methods in child classes to provide different behavior. Show the same method producing different results. Reference: https://www.typescriptlang.org/docs/
Create a collection of different types. Call the same method on all of them. Discover how each behaves differently. Reference: https://www.typescriptlang.org/docs/
Build complex polymorphic hierarchies. Show how the type determines which method actually runs. Reference: https://www.typescriptlang.org/docs/
Design multiple classes that fulfill the same interface. Show they can be used wherever that interface is expected. Reference: https://www.typescriptlang.org/docs/
Create polymorphic hierarchies with proper design. Show how polymorphism reduces coupling and increases flexibility. Reference: https://www.typescriptlang.org/docs/
Create properties and methods that belong to the class itself, not instances. Use them without creating objects. Reference: https://www.typescriptlang.org/docs/
Create a counter that tracks how many instances have been created. Store it in a static property. Reference: https://www.typescriptlang.org/docs/
Create utility functions in a class that don't need instance data. Find how to access them. Reference: https://www.typescriptlang.org/docs/
Build complex static patterns that initialize on demand. Create static factories that create instances. Reference: https://www.typescriptlang.org/docs/
Implement a singleton - a class that only has one instance. Use static members to manage it. Reference: https://www.typescriptlang.org/docs/
Design systems using static members for shared configuration and factory methods. Reference: https://www.typescriptlang.org/docs/
Write code that might fail. Catch the error and handle it gracefully. Reference: https://www.typescriptlang.org/docs/
Create custom error types by throwing different error messages. Catch them and check the error message. Reference: https://www.typescriptlang.org/docs/
Create custom error classes. Throw them when something goes wrong. Reference: https://www.typescriptlang.org/docs/
Create functions that throw errors in different situations. Use try-catch to handle them and continue execution. Reference: https://www.typescriptlang.org/docs/
Use instanceof to check error types. Handle different errors based on their type. Reference: https://www.typescriptlang.org/docs/
Design systems with proper error handling at multiple levels. Show how errors flow through the application. Reference: https://www.typescriptlang.org/docs/
Open a browser and go to https://www.google.com. Find a way to get the HTTP response status code and print it to console.
Navigate to https://www.google.com, then navigate to https://www.facebook.com. Find a way to get and print the current page URL after each navigation.
Navigate to https://www.google.com, then https://www.facebook.com. Find a way to go back to the previous page. Print the URL after going back to verify you're on Google.
Navigate to 3 different pages in sequence (Google > Facebook > Wikipedia). Find methods to go back to the first page, then forward to the last page. Print URL at each step to verify.
Navigate to https://www.google.com. Find a way to reload/refresh the page. Check if the response status code is the same before and after refresh.
Create a navigation scenario: Google > Facebook > Wikipedia > go back twice > go forward once > reload. Document all URLs and response statuses for each action. Verify you end up on the correct page.
Navigate to a page and extract page title using page.title(). Print title to console. Reference: https://playwright.dev/docs/api/class-page#page-title
Navigate to a page and get current URL using page.url(). Compare URL before and after navigation. Reference: https://playwright.dev/docs/api/class-page#page-url
Fetch full page HTML using page.content(). Extract specific data from HTML (e.g., text, attributes). Reference: https://playwright.dev/docs/api/class-page#page-content
Extract page title, URL, and content in one test. Verify all three values are consistent with page state. Reference: https://playwright.dev/docs/api/class-page
Create a page analyzer that extracts title, URL, and content, then parses content to find specific elements and data. Reference: https://playwright.dev/docs/api/class-page
Use getByRole() to find a button on https://aa-practice-test-automation.vercel.app/. Find the 'Sign In' button by its role. Reference: https://playwright.dev/docs/locators#locate-by-role
Use getByRole() to find textbox inputs. Fill a username and password field by their roles and names. Verify values were entered. Reference: https://playwright.dev/docs/locators#locate-by-role
Use getByRole() with regex pattern matching. Find a button that partially matches text (e.g., /^Sign.*/). Click it and verify action. Reference: https://playwright.dev/docs/locators#locate-by-role
Use getByRole() with exact: true option. Find inputs with exact name matches. Compare behavior with and without exact: true. Reference: https://playwright.dev/docs/locators#locate-by-role
Use getByRole() to find checkboxes. Click 'Remember me' checkbox and verify checked state. Reference: https://playwright.dev/docs/locators#locate-by-role
Use getByLabel() to find inputs by their associated label text. Fill username and password fields using labels. Reference: https://playwright.dev/docs/locators#locate-by-label
Use getByLabel() when labels are case-sensitive. Match exact label text and fill the associated input. Reference: https://playwright.dev/docs/locators#locate-by-label
Use getByLabel() with partial text matching. Find inputs where label contains (but doesn't exactly match) the search text. Reference: https://playwright.dev/docs/locators#locate-by-label
Use getByPlaceholder() to find inputs by their placeholder text. Fill 'enter your user' and 'enter your pass' fields. Reference: https://playwright.dev/docs/locators#locate-by-placeholder
Use getByPlaceholder() with exact text matching. Verify placeholder exists and fill the input. Reference: https://playwright.dev/docs/locators#locate-by-placeholder
Use getByPlaceholder() to chain multiple actions. Find input by placeholder, clear it, and fill with new value. Reference: https://playwright.dev/docs/locators#locate-by-placeholder
Use getByText() to find elements by their visible text. Find 'Welcome!' message and extract its text content. Reference: https://playwright.dev/docs/locators#locate-by-text
Use getByText() with exact matching. Find element with exact text and capture its content. Reference: https://playwright.dev/docs/locators#locate-by-text
Use getByText() with regex patterns. Find elements with partial text matching using regular expressions. Reference: https://playwright.dev/docs/locators#locate-by-text
Use getByAltText() to find images by their alt text. Find 'Fork me' image on https://the-internet.herokuapp.com/ and click it. Reference: https://playwright.dev/docs/locators#locate-by-alt-text
Use getByAltText() with complex page structures. Verify image is displayed before clicking. Reference: https://playwright.dev/docs/locators#locate-by-alt-text
Use getByTitle() to find elements by their title attribute. Find element with title='za3bola' and click it. Reference: https://playwright.dev/docs/locators#locate-by-title
Use getByTitle() to identify tooltips or help elements. Verify title attribute exists and access the element. Reference: https://playwright.dev/docs/locators#locate-by-title
Use getByTestId() with custom automation-id attribute. Configure test-id attribute using selectors.setTestIdAttribute(). Reference: https://playwright.dev/docs/locators#locate-by-test-id
Use getByTestId() to find username and password inputs. Fill them and click login button using test-ids. Reference: https://playwright.dev/docs/locators#locate-by-test-id
Use getByTestId() with custom attribute names. Change test-id attribute and verify locators work correctly. Reference: https://playwright.dev/docs/locators#locate-by-test-id

Compare all getBy() locator methods on same element. Use Role, Label, Placeholder, Text, Alt, Title, and TestId. Show which methods work best in different scenarios. Reference: https://playwright.dev/docs/locators
Build a helper function that tries multiple locator strategies (fallback pattern). Successfully find elements even if one strategy fails. Reference: https://playwright.dev/docs/locators
Use page.locator() with CSS attribute selectors. Find inputs by automation-id attribute. Fill username and password using CSS selectors. Reference: https://playwright.dev/docs/locators#css-locator
Use page.locator() with 'css=' prefix. Select button by its automation-id attribute. Click it and verify action. Reference: https://playwright.dev/docs/locators#css-locator
Use CSS selectors with various attribute matching (exact, partial, prefix). Compare behavior with getByRole and getByTestId. Reference: https://playwright.dev/docs/locators#css-locator
Combine multiple CSS selectors to find specific elements. Use attribute selectors like [attr='value']. Reference: https://playwright.dev/docs/locators#css-locator
Use page.locator() with XPath expressions. Find inputs by //input[@automation-id='username-input']. Fill the input field. Reference: https://playwright.dev/docs/locators#xpath-locator
Use page.locator() with 'xpath=' prefix. Find button by XPath and click it. Verify action completed. Reference: https://playwright.dev/docs/locators#xpath-locator
Use XPath with different axis (.//, @, text(), etc.). Find elements using various XPath strategies. Reference: https://playwright.dev/docs/locators#xpath-locator
Use XPath predicates to find specific elements by attributes and position. Compare with CSS selectors on same page. Reference: https://playwright.dev/docs/locators#xpath-locator
Compare CSS selector and XPath approaches for the same test scenario. Show pros/cons of each strategy. Reference: https://playwright.dev/docs/locators
Use locator.focus() to focus on an input field. Verify element has focus (check if focused element matches). Reference: https://playwright.dev/docs/api/class-locator#locator-focus
Use locator.blur() to remove focus from an input field. Verify element lost focus. Reference: https://playwright.dev/docs/api/class-locator#locator-blur
Focus on one input field, then blur it, then focus on another. Verify focus state changes between elements. Reference: https://playwright.dev/docs/api/class-locator#locator-focus
Use locator.check() to check a checkbox. Verify checkbox is now checked. Reference: https://playwright.dev/docs/api/class-locator#locator-check
Use locator.uncheck() to uncheck a checkbox. Verify checkbox is now unchecked. Reference: https://playwright.dev/docs/api/class-locator#locator-uncheck
Use locator.check() to select a radio button. Verify radio button is now selected. Reference: https://playwright.dev/docs/api/class-locator#locator-check
Check a checkbox, then uncheck it. Verify state changes both ways. Reference: https://playwright.dev/docs/api/class-locator#locator-check
Check multiple checkboxes on a page. Verify each one is checked after action. Reference: https://playwright.dev/docs/api/class-locator#locator-check

Topic: Playwright: Actions - Checkbox and Radio Flow
Exercise Description: Login to a page, navigate to checkbox/radio page, check checkbox, select radio button, verify both checked. Reference: https://playwright.dev/docs/input
Use locator.hover() to hover over an element. Verify hover state (e.g., tooltip appears). Reference: https://playwright.dev/docs/api/class-locator#locator-hover
Hover over an element, then perform action (click, fill). Verify interaction works after hover. Reference: https://playwright.dev/docs/api/class-locator#locator-hover
Use locator.pressSequentially() to type text character by character with delay. Verify each character appears. Reference: https://playwright.dev/docs/api/class-locator#locator-press-sequentially
Use pressSequentially() with delay option to simulate slow typing. Compare with instant fill(). Reference: https://playwright.dev/docs/api/class-locator#locator-press-sequentially
Use locator.press() to press specific keys (Enter, Escape, Tab). Verify page response to key press. Reference: https://playwright.dev/docs/api/class-locator#locator-press
Use press() with keyboard shortcuts (Control+a, Control+c, Control+v). Verify shortcut actions work. Reference: https://playwright.dev/docs/api/class-keyboard#keyboard-press

Topic: Playwright: Actions - Select Option by Index
Exercise Description: Use locator.selectOption() with index option. Select dropdown option by position. Verify correct option selected. Reference: https://playwright.dev/docs/api/class-locator#locator-select-option

Topic: Playwright: Actions - Select Option by Value
Exercise Description: Use selectOption() with value or label. Verify dropdown displays selected option. Reference: https://playwright.dev/docs/api/class-locator#locator-select-option
Use selectOption() with array of values for multi-select. Select multiple options at once. Verify all selected. Reference: https://playwright.dev/docs/api/class-locator#locator-select-option
Use locator.fill() to fill date input with format 'YYYY-MM-DD'. Verify date value in input. Reference: https://playwright.dev/docs/api/class-locator#locator-fill
Click date picker to open calendar. Navigate months using previous/next buttons. Click date number. Verify date selected. Reference: https://playwright.dev/docs/api/class-locator#locator-click
Interact with custom calendar widget: click to open, navigate months, select day. Handle calendar navigation. Reference: https://playwright.dev/docs/input#select-time
Use locator.dragTo() to drag element to target location. Verify element moved. Reference: https://playwright.dev/docs/api/class-locator#locator-drag-to

Topic: Playwright: Actions - Drag and Drop with Mouse
Exercise Description: Use mouse.down(), hover(), and mouse.up() to simulate drag-drop. Show manual mouse control. Reference: https://playwright.dev/docs/api/class-mouse

Topic: Playwright: Actions - Drag and Drop Complex
Exercise Description: Implement drag-drop with multiple steps: hover source, press, move to target, hover target, release. Reference: https://playwright.dev/docs/input#drag-and-drop
Listen for dialog event. Accept an alert dialog. Extract and log dialog message. Reference: https://playwright.dev/docs/api/class-page#page-event-dialog
Handle prompt dialog. Accept with text input. Verify page receives the input. Reference: https://playwright.dev/docs/api/class-dialog#dialog-accept
Handle confirm dialog. Dismiss (cancel) the dialog. Verify page handles dismissal. Reference: https://playwright.dev/docs/api/class-dialog#dialog-dismiss
Handle multiple dialogs in one test. Accept first dialog, dismiss second. Use page.once() and page.on(). Reference: https://playwright.dev/docs/dialogs
Capture and log dialog message. Accept or dismiss based on message content. Reference: https://playwright.dev/docs/api/class-dialog#dialog-message
Access content inside a simple iframe using frameLocator(). Fill input field within frame. Reference: https://playwright.dev/docs/frames
Use page.frameLocator() to locate and interact with iframe. Verify element inside frame. Reference: https://playwright.dev/docs/api/class-frame-locator
Access nested iframes using chained frameLocator(). Navigate through frame hierarchy. Fill input in nested frame. Reference: https://playwright.dev/docs/frames#frame-hierarchy
Interact with multiple elements across different frames. Verify actions work in each frame. Reference: https://playwright.dev/docs/frames
Use page.keyboard.press() to press keys (Enter). Verify page responds to key press. Reference: https://playwright.dev/docs/api/class-keyboard#keyboard-press
Press Enter key in input or on page. Verify form submission or action triggered. Reference: https://playwright.dev/docs/api/class-keyboard#keyboard-press
Use locator.setInputFiles() to set file for file input. Verify file is set. Reference: https://playwright.dev/docs/api/class-locator#locator-set-input-files
Use setInputFiles([]) to clear file input. Verify file was removed. Reference: https://playwright.dev/docs/api/class-locator#locator-set-input-files
Set file using full path with path.join(). Upload file and verify in page. Reference: https://playwright.dev/docs/api/class-locator#locator-set-input-files
Listen for filechooser event using page.waitForEvent(). Set files when dialog opens. Reference: https://playwright.dev/docs/api/class-page#page-wait-for-event
Use page.on('filechooser') to handle file dialog. Set files programmatically. Reference: https://playwright.dev/docs/input#upload-files
Handle multiple file uploads in sequence. Clear and re-upload files. Verify each upload. Reference: https://playwright.dev/docs/input#upload-files
Set custom test-id attribute using selectors.setTestIdAttribute('data-testid'). Access shadow element by custom test-id. Reference: https://playwright.dev/docs/locators#locate-by-test-id
Set custom test-id attribute using selectors.setTestIdAttribute('data-testid'). Access shadow element by custom test-id. Reference: https://playwright.dev/docs/locators#locate-by-test-id

Topic: Playwright: Shadow DOM - Through Page Navigation
Exercise Description: Login > navigate to shadow DOM page > find and interact with shadow DOM element. Use getByTestId() for shadow element access. Reference: https://playwright.dev/docs/shadow-dom

Topic: Playwright: Shadow DOM - Fill Shadow Input
Exercise Description: Access shadow DOM input field and fill it with text using getByTestId(). Verify text was entered. Reference: https://playwright.dev/docs/shadow-dom

Topic: Playwright: Shadow DOM - Compare with Regular DOM
Exercise Description: Interact with regular DOM elements and shadow DOM elements in same test. Show how Playwright handles both transparently. Reference: https://playwright.dev/docs/shadow-dom
